title: Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) Intrinsic Value
description: An independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) compiled entirely by Gemini 3.1.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y5)
fcfGrowth.rationale:
Microsoft's aggressive capital expenditures in AI data centers are currently suppressing Free Cash Flow
            growth slightly below their explosive revenue growth. However, a 12% growth rate is highly conservative
            given the near-monopoly pricing power of Office 365 Copilot and Azure's immense scalability over the next five years.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale:
Using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). With the US 10-Year Treasury yield at 4.18%, a beta of 1.2,
            and an estimated equity risk premium of 4.5%, Microsoft's cost of equity lands near 9.5%. Factoring in
            their AAA-equivalent debt profile slightly lowers their Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC) to 8.8%.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale:
A 3.5% terminal growth rate assumes Microsoft outpaces global GDP inflation perpetually. Given the ultimate
            stickiness of enterprise software ecosystems, escaping Microsoft's gravity is virtually impossible for
            modern corporations, justifying a premium terminal rate.

verdictFooter: Based on FY2025 actuals. Data via S&P Global (Mar 18, 2026).

items.title: AI Capital Expenditure Drag
items.description: Microsoft is spending tens of billions on AI GPUs and data centers. If the ROI on these investments takes longer than anticipated, Free Cash Flow margins will compress severely over the next 3-5 years.

items.title: Regulatory Scrutiny
items.description: As an undisputed tech monopoly, ongoing antitrust pressures in the EU and US could force structural changes or massive fines, hampering growth.

items.title: Macroeconomic Sensitivity
items.description: While enterprise software is sticky, a severe global recession would still impact Azure consumption growth and seat-license expansions.

faq.question: Why use a 12% FCF growth rate for Microsoft?
faq.answer: While Microsoft's revenue may grow faster than 12%, Free Cash Flow is constrained by record-breaking capital expenditures required to build AI infrastructure (data centers, Nvidia GPUs). 12% represents a balanced view where revenue scales aggressively but margins face near-term pressure.

faq.question: Does Microsoft's debt hurt its intrinsic value?
faq.answer: No. Microsoft operates with a net cash position. They hold significantly more cash and short-term investments than total debt. In our model, this net cash surplus (approx. $33,967 million) is added directly to the enterprise value.

faq.question: What makes Microsoft's terminal rate higher than GDP?
faq.answer: A standard company's terminal rate shouldn't exceed inflation/GDP (usually 2-2.5%). However, Microsoft's deep integration into the global economy via Windows, Active Directory, and Azure gives them quasi-sovereign pricing power, allowing them to perpetually raise prices faster than inflation.
